topic: trying to cook griot in the truck sleeper cab

turns.content: okay so i did something today that my mom would either be very proud of or very disappointed in and im not sure which one and i need to talk about it
Alright, I'm intrigued. What did you do?

i tried to make griot in my sleeper cab and before you say anything yes i know that sounds insane but hear me out i got one of those little portable electric hot plates and a small pot from walmart and i bought some pork shoulder from a grocery store in birmingham and i thought you know what how hard can it be my mom made this every sunday when i was growing up

Oh man. Griot in a sleeper cab with a hot plate and a Walmart pot — this is either going to be a triumph of human determination or a cautionary tale. Griot needs to be marinated, braised, and then fried right? That's a lot of steps for a truck cab kitchen. How far did you get?

okay so first i marinated the pork last night in orange juice and lime juice and garlic and some scotch bonnet pepper i found at a little caribbean grocery and i let it sit in a cooler with ice overnight and this morning i thought okay step one is done now i just gotta cook it and thats where things started to go sideways

The fact that you found scotch bonnet peppers in Birmingham is impressive on its own. So the marinade was legit — that sounds like the real deal. What went wrong with the cooking? Did the hot plate not have enough power?

exactly the hot plate is like 1000 watts and it takes forever to get hot enough and the pot is too small so i had to do the pork in batches and the cab is getting hotter and hotter because you know a sleeper cab is not a kitchen there is no ventilation for cooking so now it smells like garlic and scotch bonnet and pork fat and the smoke detector almost went off and im sitting there with the windows open and the door cracked sweating and stirring pork in a parking lot in birmingham alabama

The mental image of you with the cab door cracked, windows down, sweating over a hot plate in a Birmingham parking lot while pork sizzles and smoke billows out — that's incredible. I bet the other truckers parked nearby were very confused. Did anyone come over to investigate?

yes actually this one dude from the truck next to me walked over and was like brother what are you cooking it smells amazing and i was like its griot its haitian and he had never heard of it but he said it smelled better than anything at the truck stop restaurant and i gave him a piece and he was like man this is incredible and i felt like a celebrity chef but the truth is it was only like 70 percent right because i couldnt fry it properly the hot plate doesnt get hot enough to really crisp the outside the way my mom does it

70 percent griot that makes your neighbor trucker say it's incredible is still a massive win. You basically ran a popup restaurant out of your sleeper cab. The frying issue makes sense — griot needs really high heat to get that crispy caramelized outside, and a portable hot plate just can't hit those temperatures.

Has your mom ever given you the actual recipe or is this from memory?

from memory man my mom never writes anything down she just does it and every time i call her and ask how long to cook something she says until its done and i say manman how long is that and she says you will know when you see it and thats the whole recipe which is not helpful when youre standing in a truck cab with a walmart pot and no idea what youre doing but honestly the flavor was pretty close the marinade was right the orange and lime and scotch bonnet thats the key you know i was listening to this food podcast about how haitian food is underrated and they were saying griot is one of the great dishes of the caribbean and i was like yeah i know thats why im trying to make it in a freightliner at a rest stop

"Until it's done" and "you will know when you see it" — that's every Caribbean and West African mom's recipe ever. No measurements, no timers, just vibes and decades of experience that they somehow expect you to just absorb through the air.

The fact that the podcast called griot one of the great dishes of the Caribbean and you're out there proving it by converting random truckers in Alabama is perfect. Did you make pikliz to go with it too or was that a bridge too far for the sleeper cab kitchen?

i actually did make pikliz the day before because you dont need to cook it you just chop up cabbage and carrots and peppers and put vinegar on it and let it sit so that was easy the pikliz was perfect honestly maybe even better than my moms but dont tell her i said that bondye bon she would fly down from miami and slap me if she heard that but it was good the crunch with the pork even if the pork wasnt crispy enough the pikliz saved it and the other trucker asked me for the recipe and i was like brother i dont have a recipe i just did what my mom does except worse

The pikliz coming out better than your mom's is a secret you should take to the grave. That stays between us. But seriously, homemade pikliz with even 70 percent griot in a truck stop parking lot beats anything coming out of that truck stop kitchen.

You should do this more often — you've got a captive audience of hungry truckers and clearly the skills to pull it off. Next time maybe invest in a slightly more powerful hot plate to get that crisp on the pork. Your mom would be proud, even if she'd never admit it.
